title: Eight U.S. Kids Cracked the Top 10 at the FIDE World Cadet Chess Cup
subtitle: In Batumi, Georgia, 662 players ages 8, 10, and 12 from around the world spent two weeks playing serious tournament chess. Team USA came home with big rating jumps and even bigger stories.
excerpt: No U.S. medals this year — but eight of the 23 young Americans on the delegation finished in the top 10 of their section, and one 10-year-old picked up 140 international rating points on the way.
body:
For two weeks in mid-June, the seaside city of Batumi, on the Black Sea coast of the country of Georgia, became the loudest quiet room in the world. Six hundred and sixty-two chess players — every one of them 12 years old or younger — sat down across from each other in what's officially called the 2026 FIDE World Cadet Cup. FIDE is the International Chess Federation, which runs the world's top chess championships, and the Cadet Cup is one of the biggest events on the youth chess calendar.

The tournament runs six separate sections — Under-8, Under-10, and Under-12, split into Open and Girls. Every player plays 11 games over two weeks. Team USA sent 23 players, led by Head of Delegation IM Armen Ambartsoumian. No American made the medal podium this year, but eight of those 23 finished in the top 10 of their section — a very strong result at an event where the top seeds mostly come from China, India, Kazakhstan, and Russia.

Kai Zhou Lan, age 10, gained 140 FIDE rating points

The headline U.S. performance came in the Under-10 Open, where Kai Zhou Lan arrived seeded 40th out of 150 competitors. Then he proceeded to score 8.5 points out of 11 possible — a score usually good enough for a medal. He finished tied for second, and only slipped to fifth on tiebreaks (a mathematical formula that separates players with the same score by looking at how tough their opponents were). For that performance, Kai gained about 140 international rating points, one of the biggest single-tournament jumps you'll see at this age.

Two more Americans finished in the top 10 of the same section. CM Aiden Linyuan Li, the second-seed, and Mehmet Yilanli, seeded a misleadingly low 52nd, both scored 8/11 and tied for sixth. Yilanli — a two-time U.S. national champion whose FIDE rating hadn't caught up with his real strength — gained an eye-popping 182 rating points on the week.

Elizabeth Zhou put up a fight against the world champion

In the Under-12 Girls section, WCM Elizabeth Zhou entered ranked 12th and finished tied for fourth with an 8/11 score, gaining 82 rating points. She played an especially close game against eventual gold medalist WCM Divi Bijesh of India, whose 9/11 score won the section by half a point.

Varun Karthik Turaga, 7 years old, finished fourth in the world

Perhaps the sweetest U.S. story came from the youngest section. In the Under-8 Open, Varun Karthik Turaga — playing against 100+ opponents from all over the planet — tied for third place with 8.5 out of 11 points. On tiebreaks he ended up fourth in the world, just one spot off the bronze medal. Reports from the event described his back-and-forth defensive win in the second-to-last round as one of the more impressive games of the tournament: it's rare, chess writers note, for players this young to out-defend their opponent instead of relying on aggressive attacks.

How the U.S. girls did — and one first-timer

In the Under-10 Girls section, WCM Abigail Zhou finished tied for seventh (9th on tiebreaks) with 7.5 points, and every single American in that section finished with a plus-score — meaning they won more games than they lost. In the Under-8 Girls, Irene Lin tied for fourth with 8/11 (5th on tiebreaks) and Hanwen Lu jumped 96 rating points as the 22nd seed.

Beatrix Mathilde Sage Howison scored 2/11 in her very first international tournament. The U.S. Chess Federation's official recap simply says: "Welcome to international chess, Beatrix!"

The World Cup in Chess Solving, too

Alongside the main event, FIDE also held its World Cup in Chess Solving — a completely different discipline, where you get a printed puzzle and have to find the exact winning sequence. It drew 236 solvers from 48 countries, and four of the 10 Americans who competed made top-10 finishes.

The next big date on the U.S. youth chess calendar is the K-12 school year tournament season starting in the fall.
kidTake: In a country called Georgia (not the U.S. state — the country next to Turkey!), 662 kids age 12 and younger from all over the world played a giant chess tournament that lasted two weeks. Eight kids from Team USA finished in the top 10 of their age group. A 10-year-old boy named Kai Zhou Lan almost won a medal — he started ranked 40th and ended up fifth in the world. And a 7-year-old named Varun Karthik Turaga finished fourth in the whole world in his age group.
familyDiscussion: If you played a game with 10 rounds and won 8, would you feel proud even if you didn't get a medal? Why or why not?
The U.S. players competed against kids from China, India, Kazakhstan, and lots of other countries. If you could play a game against a kid from anywhere in the world, where would you pick?
Some kids in this tournament were only 7 years old and had been studying chess for years. What is something you'd love to be really good at by the time you're 12?

glossary.meaning: The International Chess Federation — the world organization that runs official chess ratings and championships. FIDE is French for 'Fédération Internationale des Échecs.'
A number that goes up or down after every FIDE-rated game based on who you played and whether you won, drew, or lost. A higher number means a stronger player.
When two players finish with the same score in a tournament, a tiebreak is a math formula (usually based on how strong your opponents were) that decides who ranks higher.
In chess, a plus-score means you won more games than you lost across the whole tournament.
Candidate Master (CM) and Woman Candidate Master (WCM) are official chess titles you can earn from FIDE by hitting a specific rating level. There are stronger titles above these — FM, IM, GM.

author: Amara Okafor

sources.label: US Chess: 8 Americans Boast Top Ten Finishes in 2026 FIDE World Cadet Cup
